What are the best tools for generating images?
Comparing the options for photorealistic images

brief.target_reader: Independent content creators, social media producers, and visual artists who need photorealistic AI-generated images for commercial or editorial use and want to choose a tool without wading through marketing copy
brief.reader_outcome: The reader can identify which photorealistic image generation tool fits their specific workflow, budget, and output quality requirements, and know the practical tradeoffs before committing time or money

claims_to_verify: Current pricing tiers and credit systems for Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, DALL-E 3 (via ChatGPT and API), Stable Diffusion (SDXL and SD3), Flux.1, and Ideogram as of the article publish date
Which tools offer commercial licensing and under what plan tiers
Whether Midjourney allows API access or is still Discord/web-only
Flux.1 model variants (Pro, Dev, Schnell) and their actual availability through third-party platforms vs. direct access
Adobe Firefly's Content Credentials and IP-safe training claims, and any independent verification of those claims
DALL-E 3 native resolution and upscaling options
Stable Diffusion 3 release status and any access restrictions
Which tools support inpainting, outpainting, and image-to-image workflows natively
Ideogram's current typography and photorealism capabilities relative to stated version (Ideogram 2.0 or later)
Any known content policy restrictions that would affect commercial creative use (e.g., likeness of real people, nudity policies)
brief.research_plan: Prioritize official documentation and pricing pages for each tool for accuracy on cost and licensing. Look for independent technical evaluations and benchmark comparisons from AI art and photography communities (e.g., community posts on Reddit r/StableDiffusion, Midjourney Discord feedback threads, or creator YouTube channels that do side-by-side prompt tests). Avoid relying on the tools' own marketing pages for capability claims. For commercial licensing, go directly to terms of service pages. For real-world output quality, find creator forums or published prompt comparisons with visible outputs across identical or near-identical prompts. Note publication dates carefully since model versions update frequently.

submitted.idea: Quick start guide for a traditional music incorporating AI into their music

brief.target_reader: Traditional musicians (folk, classical, jazz, world music, etc.) who have little or no prior experience with AI tools but are curious about incorporating AI into their creative workflow without losing the authenticity or cultural integrity of their practice
brief.reader_outcome: The reader should be able to identify which AI tools are relevant to their specific workflow, set up at least one practical AI-assisted process (transcription, arrangement, practice, or composition aid), and understand the tradeoffs of each approach before committing time or money
structure_plan: What This Guide Is (and Is Not): Scope and honest expectations
Where AI Actually Fits in a Traditional Musician's Workflow
Step 1: Transcription and Notation. Tools that convert audio recordings to sheet music or tablature
Step 2: Practice and Ear Training. AI tools for feedback, tuning analysis, and learning by ear
Step 3: Arrangement and Orchestration Assistance. Using AI to explore harmonizations or instrumentation without replacing your musical judgment
Step 4: Audio Production and Mixing. AI-assisted noise reduction, stem separation, and mastering for traditional recordings
Step 5: Finding and Organizing Reference Material. AI search and cataloging tools for traditional repertoire
Tradeoffs and Honest Limitations: Where AI falls short for traditional music specifically
Suggested Starter Workflows by Instrument or Genre
Tools at a Glance: Comparison table with use case, cost, and platform
claims_to_verify: Current pricing and free tier limits for Transcribe+ (or similar audio-to-notation tools)
Current pricing and accuracy claims for Sibelius AudioScore, Notion AI, or AnthemScore for polyphonic traditional instruments
Whether iReal Pro or similar tools have AI-assisted features or remain rule-based
Capabilities and current pricing of Moises, Lalal.ai, or similar stem separation tools
Whether tools like Google Magenta or similar open-source options support non-Western scales and microtonal tuning
Current status and pricing of Musecore's AI transcription features
Whether Adobe Podcast Enhance or similar tools handle acoustic folk/traditional instruments well or are optimized for speech
Whether any major AI composition tools (Suno, Udio, etc.) can be prompted to stay within a traditional style without generating plagiarism or stylistic distortion
Data privacy and ownership policies for audio uploaded to cloud-based AI tools, particularly for unpublished traditional recordings
Whether any AI ear training platforms (Tonara, Soundslice, etc.) offer feedback for non-Western or modal tuning systems
brief.research_plan: Prioritize hands-on user reviews and forum discussions from communities of traditional musicians (Celtic, Appalachian, Indian classical, jazz, etc.) on Reddit, Slashdot Music, and specialist forums over vendor marketing material. Check AudioMelody, Sound on Sound, and similar practitioner-oriented publications for tool comparisons. For transcription accuracy, look for independent benchmark comparisons of audio-to-notation tools on polyphonic and ornamented sources, not just piano. For cultural integrity concerns, look for commentary from ethnomusicologists or traditional music educators who have written about AI use. Verify all pricing and feature claims directly from vendor sites at time of research and note the date, as these change frequently.

sources.title: AnthemScore Official Product Page (via Lunaverus/Upwork/BestAITools)
claims_supported: AnthemScore uses a neural network/machine learning to convert MP3 and WAV audio files into sheet music or guitar tab
AnthemScore offers a 30-day free trial (generates up to 30 seconds of sheet music in trial mode)
Pricing tiers (as of 2025–2026): Lite ~$31, Professional ~$42, Studio ~$107 — all one-time purchases, no subscription
AnthemScore excels with single-instrument or simple tracks but may need manual edits for polyphonic or niche genres
Runs fully offline on Windows, Mac, and Linux; processes pre-recorded audio only, not real-time
Exports to PDF, MusicXML, and MIDI

sources.title: iReal Pro Official Website
claims_supported: iReal Pro explicitly states 'No AI' on its homepage — all backing track styles are hand-crafted by real musicians from programmed MIDI loops or live-recorded drums
iReal Pro is a chord-chart and backing-track tool, not an AI tool — it is entirely rule-based/programmer-crafted
iReal Pro is a one-time purchase with no subscription; each platform (iOS, Android, Mac) is a separate purchase
Actively developed for 18 years; runs fully offline
Offers 50+ backing track styles; used at Berklee College of Music and other top music schools
App price is approximately $19.99–$21.99 depending on platform (price increased to $21.99 on Android in Dec 2025)

sources.title: Moises Official Website (moises.ai)
claims_supported: Moises explicitly states it will never train on user-uploaded data
Moises only trains models on licensed materials and never trains on any music without the owner's full approval
Moises offers a free tier with a limited number of uploads per month
Moises has been recognized as a 2025 Apple Design Awards Finalist, 2024 iPad App of the Year Winner, and 2025 Microsoft Store Awards Winner
Moises is trusted by 70 million musicians worldwide (company claim)

sources.title: Moises AI Pricing and Feature Breakdown (multiple independent reviews, 2025–2026)
sources.publisher: StemSplit / AI Music Preneur / SunoWatermark / Chartlex
claims_supported: Moises free tier: 5 track separations per month, basic stems (vocals + instrumental), ads between jobs, no credit card required

Moises Premium: $3.99/month (billed monthly) or ~$2.99/month billed annually (~$35.88/year) — unlocks 30 uploads/month, all stem types including granular splits, tempo change with pitch lock, pitch shift, key detection, lyric transcription
Moises Pro: $9.99/month or ~$89.99/year — unlimited uploads, highest-quality models, API access
Moises separates vocals, drums, bass, guitar, piano (up to 5-stem splits on Premium/Pro); free tier limited to basic 2-stem separation
Moises strength is the all-in-one practice experience (chord detection, speed control, metronome, transposition) combined with stem separation — not raw separation quality alone
LALAL.AI is credit-based (starting ~$10 for 90 minutes of processing), not subscription-based, and is considered the stronger pure separator for vocals on modern pop/electronic mixes
Moises processes audio in the cloud; a connection is required to separate or generate new audio; offline use on higher tiers available only for already-processed tracks
Pricing figures vary slightly across third-party sources and may be region-dependent — writers should verify directly at moises.ai

sources.title: Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech — Official Product Page and Independent Reviews
claims_supported: Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech (now v2) is explicitly marketed as an AI filter for spoken audio — its own product page describes it as an 'AI filter for cleaning up spoken audio' and as 'ideal for podcasters, content creators, and professionals'
Enhance Speech v2 is currently free with no Adobe subscription required for the basic tier
Independent reviewers explicitly flag it as 'less suitable for music audio — optimized for speech, not instrument recordings'

The tool uses AI to identify sections with human speech versus silence/noise; AGC adjusts volume levels to maintain consistent loudness — designed for voice, not acoustic instrument timbre preservation
Over-processing risk: can make voice sound slightly artificial at high strength settings; 'V2 can feel all-or-nothing on slider'
Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech v2 was updated in June 2025 and is accessible directly within Studio

sources.title: Suno and Udio Copyright Litigation and Settlements (Forbes / Music Business Worldwide / Brooklyn Law / 404 Media)
sources.publisher: Forbes / Music Business Worldwide / Brooklyn Sports & Entertainment Law Blog / 404 Media
claims_supported: Suno and Udio were sued in June 2024 by all three major record labels (UMG, WMG, Sony) for training on copyrighted recordings without authorization

Partial settlements reached: UMG settled with Udio (Oct 2025), WMG settled with both Udio (Nov 2025) and Suno (Nov 2025); Sony remains in litigation with both companies; UMG continues its lawsuit against Suno as of early 2026

Users could (and can) prompt these platforms to generate songs 'in the style of' specific artists — the labels demonstrated outputs that replicated distinctive rhythms, melodies, and stylistic signatures of famous songs

A June 2025 U.S. Copyright Office report stated that 'the fair use doctrine does not excuse unauthorized training on expressive works (e.g., music) particularly when those works are used to generate substitutional outputs'
Suno's own legal defense argues its outputs 'exclusively generate new sounds' rather than samples, but this is a contested legal claim under active litigation — not a settled fact

Independent artists (Tony Justice, 5th Wheel Records) filed additional class action lawsuits in June 2025 seeking to represent all independent artists whose work appeared on streaming services since January 2021

Stylistic distortion risk is real and documented: when prompted with specific stylistic cues (artist names, genre descriptors), both platforms have produced outputs strikingly similar to protected recordings
Traditional/folk artists uploading unpublished recordings to these platforms for prompting carry meaningful copyright and IP risk given the unresolved litigation landscape

sources.title: Suno Copyright Filter Bypass Investigation
claims_supported: Suno's copyright protection filters are reportedly trivial to bypass with minimal effort, as documented by The Verge's investigation
Even with copyright filters in place, Suno has produced AI-generated imitations of iconic songs (Beyoncé, Black Sabbath, Aqua) that sound alarmingly close to the originals
Suno's filter vulnerabilities undermine the platform's claims to responsible self-policing

sources.title: AnthemScore Accuracy — User Reviews (SourceForge / Slashdot / BestAITools)
claims_supported: Recurring user feedback that AnthemScore accuracy on polyphonic or 'niche genre' audio is significantly weaker than on solo piano or simple instrumental tracks — users report rhythmically displaced notes, extra artifact notes, and poor handling of unconventional recordings
Official review site (BestAITools) independently confirms: 'It excels with simple tracks but may need manual edits for polyphonic or niche genres'
AnthemScore performs best with high-quality recordings that minimize noise; internally resamples all audio to 44100 Hz

sources.title: Microtonal Music Software Landscape (The Microtone Fox, Medium, 2025–2026)
claims_supported: Most mainstream DAWs and AI music tools assume 12-tone equal temperament (12-TET) by default; getting them to work with other tunings requires native support, clever routing, plugin-based solutions, or combinations thereof

The field of microtonal software in 2025 is largely composed of specialized DAW plugins and notation tools (e.g., Entonal Studio, Scala .scl format), not AI composition assistants — the major AI composition tools (Suno, Udio, etc.) are not documented as supporting microtonal or non-Western scale output
No AI composition tool (Suno, Udio, Magenta) is documented in available sources as explicitly supporting non-Western scales or microtonal tuning in its output generation

sources.title: iReal Pro Version History (Official)
claims_supported: iReal Pro's most recent updates (2025) have added features like real drum recordings for Jazz styles, double-time options, Brazilian style packs, and accessibility improvements — none introduce AI features
All iReal Pro style updates are described in terms of hand-crafted musician programming (MIDI or live drum recordings), not AI generation

unverifiable_claims: Transcribe+ (Seventh String Software — the audio-to-notation tool specifically marketed to musicians) pricing and free tier limits: Search results returned Amazon Transcribe and transcribe.com (an unrelated speech-to-text SaaS), not Seventh String's Transcribe+. Pricing for Seventh String's Transcribe+ could not be verified from available search results. Writer must check https://www.seventhstring.com/xscribe/overview.html directly.

Sibelius AudioScore current pricing and accuracy claims for polyphonic traditional instruments: No current (2025–2026) independent pricing or accuracy data was surfaced for Sibelius AudioScore specifically. Writer should check Avid's official site directly.

Notion AI (the music notation app's AI features) — current pricing and accuracy for polyphonic traditional instruments: Notion (by Presonus/Fender) AI transcription feature status and pricing could not be verified from search results. Writer should check https://www.presonus.com/en-US/software/notion.html directly.

MuseScore's AI transcription features — current status and pricing: No current (2025–2026) authoritative source confirming whether MuseScore has launched AI transcription features was returned. Writer should check https://musescore.com directly.

Google Magenta's support for non-Western scales and microtonal tuning: No direct, authoritative 2025 source on Magenta's microtonal/non-Western scale capabilities was found. Available sources confirm the general software landscape problem (12-TET assumption), but Magenta's specific current feature set could not be confirmed. Writer should check https://magenta.tensorflow.org directly.

Whether Adobe Podcast Enhance handles acoustic folk/traditional instruments 'well' — qualitative performance on folk instruments: Sources confirm Enhance Speech is speech-optimized and 'less suitable for music audio,' but no independent test specifically using traditional/folk acoustic instruments (fiddle, oud, tin whistle, etc.) was found. The speech-optimization finding is verified; the degree of degradation on specific folk timbres is not.

Tonara, Soundslice, or any AI ear training platform offering feedback for non-Western or modal tuning systems: No current source verifying or refuting this claim was returned. Writer should either drop this claim or verify directly with each platform.

Whether any major AI composition tool (Suno, Udio, etc.) can be 'safely prompted to stay within a traditional style without generating plagiarism or stylistic distortion': The litigation evidence clearly shows stylistic bleed and copyright risk when prompting with specific stylistic descriptors. However, there is no controlled study or authoritative review specifically testing prompting for non-Western or traditional folk styles. The general risk is verified; the specific performance on traditional/folk prompts is not.

LALAL.AI specific current pricing: Third-party sources cite figures ($10 starter pack for 90 minutes, $15/month subscription figures), but these conflict across sources and may be outdated. Writer should verify at https://www.lalal.ai/pricing directly.

Data privacy and ownership policies for audio uploaded to cloud-based AI tools beyond Moises: Only Moises's data policy ('will never train on your data') was directly verified via official source. Privacy/ownership policies for Suno, Udio, LALAL.AI, Adobe Podcast Enhance, AnthemScore (offline, so moot), and others were not verified from authoritative policy pages in this search session. Writer should link directly to each tool's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.

claims_to_verify: Current pricing and free tier limits for Midjourney (subscription tiers, monthly image caps)

brief.research_plan: Prioritize official pricing and policy pages for Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, and OpenAI DALL-E 3 to verify all claims about cost, access, and rights. Use recent (within 6 months) creator community sources such as Reddit communities for practical beginner experience reports. Look for structured prompt guides from working designers or illustrators rather than AI company marketing material. Avoid aggregator listicles that repackage press releases. For limitations, look for documented failure cases shared by practitioners, not theoretical critiques.

sources.title: Comparing Midjourney Plans
claims_supported: Midjourney offers four subscription tiers: Basic, Standard, Pro, and Mega
Standard, Pro, and Mega plans include unlimited image generations via Relax Mode; Basic does not
Stealth Mode (private generation) is only available on Pro and Mega plans
Annual plans receive a 20% discount vs. monthly billing
Paid subscribers are free to use their images/videos in 'just about any way you want'
Companies making more than $1,000,000 USD in gross revenue per year must purchase the Pro or Mega plan for commercial use

sources.title: Web vs Discord – Midjourney
claims_supported: Midjourney subscribers can create images on both the website and Discord — a standalone web interface exists
Discord is no longer required; both interfaces are officially supported

sources.title: Midjourney pricing in 2026: Plans, GPU hours, and what it actually costs
claims_supported: Midjourney Basic: $10/month ($8/month annual); Standard: $30/month ($24/month annual); Pro: $60/month ($48/month annual); Mega: $120/month ($96/month annual)
Midjourney removed its free trial in March 2023 — there is no free tier
Standard plan includes unlimited Relax mode for image generation (slower queue, no Fast GPU cost)
Basic plan has no Relax mode — all generations draw from the 200-minute Fast cap (~200 images/month)
Companies with gross annual revenue over $1,000,000 must use Pro or Mega for commercial use

sources.title: Midjourney Web V7 vs. Discord: Is the New Interface Ready for Pros?
claims_supported: As of March 2026, Midjourney still offers no public API
The web interface launched as an alpha in August 2024 and became the recommended experience by April 2025 (V7 launch)
Both web and Discord interfaces access the same underlying model; image quality is identical regardless of interface

sources.title: Generative Credits FAQ | Creative Cloud
claims_supported: Creative Cloud subscription plans include a monthly allocation of generative credits usable across Firefly and CC apps
Free plan generative credits are allocated upon first use of a Firefly feature and expire one month from allocation
If you have a Creative Cloud Pro, Firefly, or credits plan, you have unlimited access to standard generations — credits are only spent on premium generative features
As of February 12, 2025, legacy Firefly and Generative Credits add-on plans are no longer available for new purchases

sources.title: Adobe Firefly – Free Generative AI for Creatives (Product Page FAQ)
claims_supported: Adobe Firefly has a free plan with limited generative credits
Adobe does not train Firefly models on Creative Cloud subscribers' personal content
The Firefly models are trained on licensed content and public domain content where copyright has expired
Adobe Firefly Custom Models allow training on your own assets

sources.title: Adobe Firefly Pricing 2026: Plans, Hidden Costs & Cheaper Alternatives
claims_supported: Firefly free tier provides 25 credits/month — approximately 25 standard image generations
Firefly Standard standalone plan: $9.99/month with 2,000 premium credits; Firefly Pro: $19.99/month with 4,000 premium credits
Creative Cloud All Apps includes approximately 1,000 Firefly credits/month; single-app CC plans include approximately 500/month
Free tier output is noted as watermarked and low-resolution and not suitable for commercial or professional use
Standard generations (basic resolution) are unlimited on all paid plans; credits are consumed by premium features like high-resolution output, video, and partner models
Credits do not roll over — they reset monthly on billing date

sources.title: Adobe Firefly Pricing: Plans, Credits, Promo and API
claims_supported: As of August 2025, the Adobe Creative Cloud 'All Apps Plan' was discontinued and replaced by Creative Cloud Pro and Creative Cloud Standard
Creative Cloud Pro is the only CC plan that includes access to Firefly premium features without a separate Firefly subscription
The Firefly plan (standalone) gives access to premium features including AI video generation, partner models (ChatGPT Image, Google Veo 3)

sources.title: ChatGPT + DALL-E 2026: What Changed & Free Limits
claims_supported: DALL-E 3 was removed from the ChatGPT interface in December 2025 and replaced with GPT-4o native image generation; 'ChatGPT image generation' now means GPT-4o output, not DALL-E 3
DALL-E 3 remains available through the OpenAI API, with deprecation announced for May 2026
ChatGPT free tier includes limited GPT-4o image generation with dynamic usage caps; OpenAI does not publish a fixed image quota
ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) raises image generation limits and adds priority processing, but the exact daily image quota is not publicly documented
OpenAI API charges per image rather than applying a hidden cap — providing a guaranteed quota path for developers

sources.title: AI Art Commercial Use FAQ: Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion Rights
claims_supported: Midjourney's Terms of Service (Section 4) grant commercial usage rights only to paid subscribers; free trial users have no commercial rights
Basic, Standard, and Pro Midjourney subscribers under $1M annual revenue can use generated images commercially
Companies exceeding the $1M revenue threshold must purchase the Pro or Mega plan for commercial use
OpenAI grants full commercial rights to DALL-E (and by extension GPT Image) outputs, subject to content policy
Adobe Firefly free tier is not licensed for commercial use; paid plans unlock commercial rights

unverifiable_claims: Exact maximum output resolution on the free tier for each tool — official Adobe, Midjourney, and OpenAI documentation reviewed does not publish specific pixel-dimension caps per tier; third-party sources describe free-tier Firefly output as 'standard resolution' but do not give pixel dimensions, and Midjourney/ChatGPT free-tier resolution caps are not publicly documented in detail. Writer should soften to 'standard resolution' or omit specific pixel numbers without official sourcing.

Exact number of free monthly credits on the current Adobe Firefly free plan — third-party sources cite 25 credits/month, but the official Adobe product page states only 'limited free credits' without specifying the exact number; the 25-credit figure should be treated as a company-period claim pending verification against the current Adobe Firefly plans page directly.

Whether ChatGPT free tier currently offers DALL-E 3 specifically — multiple independent sources confirm that DALL-E 3 was replaced by GPT-4o native image generation in ChatGPT in December 2025, so references to 'DALL-E 3 via ChatGPT' for current users are outdated. Writer should frame this as 'OpenAI image generation via ChatGPT (formerly DALL-E 3, now GPT-4o / GPT Image 1.5)' and note the API deprecation of DALL-E 3 as of May 2026.

Exact DALL-E 3 API per-image pricing breakdown — third-party sources cite $0.04 (standard) to $0.12 (HD) per image, but the official OpenAI pricing page returned insufficient detail in this search to confirm current rates. These figures should be verified directly at platform.openai.com/docs/pricing before publishing, as OpenAI has been actively transitioning to GPT Image models with different pricing.

Whether Adobe Firefly's free tier images carry a visible watermark — one third-party source (ToolRadar) states this, but it is not confirmed by Adobe's official documentation reviewed; writer should soften or verify directly.

Exact Firefly credit allocation included with Creative Cloud All Apps / Pro plans — sources conflict (ToolRadar states 1,000/month for All Apps; gptprompts.ai states 4,000/month matching Firefly Pro). This is likely because the 'All Apps' plan was discontinued in August 2025 and replaced by CC Pro/Standard. Writer must check current Adobe CC Pro plan specs directly before citing a specific number.

article.title: AI Image Generation: A Quick-Start Guide for Creators
alternative_titles: How to Get Started with AI Image Generation (No Coding Required)
Your First AI Image: A Practical Guide for Artists and Designers
article.dek: What you actually need to start generating images with AI, including which tool to pick, how to write a prompt that works, and the mistakes that waste most beginners' time.
article.meta_description: A practical quick-start guide to AI image generation for artists, designers, and independent creators. Covers tool selection, prompt writing, basic settings, iteration, and honest limitations.
article.quick_answer: To generate your first AI image, pick one tool (Adobe Firefly is the most accessible free starting point for creators with copyright concerns), write a short descriptive prompt with a subject, style, and mood, then iterate by adding or changing one element at a time.
article.introduction:
AI image generation has gone from novelty to a tool working designers and filmmakers use in actual production pipelines. If you have not tried it yet, the gap between what you have heard and what these tools can actually do is worth closing with a few minutes of hands-on use.

This guide is for creators who have never opened an image generation tool. It skips the hype and covers what you need to get a usable result: the right tool for your situation, a prompt framework that works, the settings that matter, and a realistic picture of where these tools fall short.

sections.heading: What You Actually Need to Get Started
sections.body_markdown:
You do not need special hardware, a GPU, or any coding background. All three tools covered here run entirely in your browser.

What you do need:

- A stable internet connection
- An account on whichever platform you choose
- A small budget, or willingness to work within free-tier constraints
- About 20 minutes for your first session

**On cost:** None of the major tools offers a fully unlimited free plan. Free tiers exist for Adobe Firefly and ChatGPT, but Midjourney has no free tier at all (its free trial was removed in 2023). Budget $10 to $30 per month if you plan to use any of these tools seriously. If you just want to evaluate them, Adobe Firefly's free plan and ChatGPT's free tier (which includes limited OpenAI image generation) are reasonable starting points before you commit to a paid subscription.

sections.heading: Choosing Your First Tool
sections.body_markdown:
Three tools are worth your attention as a beginner. Each has a genuinely different profile.

**Midjourney** produces visually polished outputs and is widely used by professional illustrators and concept artists. It now has a standalone web interface in addition to its original Discord workflow, so Discord is no longer required. There is no free trial (the free trial was removed in early 2023 and has not returned): the entry price is $10/month (or $8/month billed annually) for the Basic plan. According to eesel.ai, the Basic plan includes a 200-GPU-minute fast generation cap per month, which works out to roughly 200 images under typical settings, though actual generation counts vary by model and output parameters. Standard ($30/month, or $24 annual) and above add unlimited "Relax Mode" generations, which are slower but have no hard cap. The Basic plan does not include Relax Mode. Paid subscribers can use images commercially; companies with over $1 million in annual gross revenue must be on the Pro or Mega plan for commercial use.

**Adobe Firefly** is the most accessible entry point for creators who already use Creative Cloud or who have copyright concerns. Adobe states that Firefly is trained on licensed content and public domain content where copyright has expired, and that Adobe does not train on subscribers' personal content. The free plan includes a limited monthly credit allocation. A third-party review (ToolRadar) cites approximately 25 credits per month on the free tier, but Adobe's own documentation describes it only as "limited" without specifying a number, so treat that figure as an estimate. Free-tier outputs are not licensed for commercial use, and ToolRadar notes they may also be watermarked and at lower resolution than paid-tier output. Paid plans unlock commercial rights. Standard generations are unlimited on paid plans; credits are spent on premium features like high-resolution output and video. A third-party source (ToolRadar) cites the standalone Firefly plan starting at $9.99/month, though this figure has not been independently verified against Adobe's current pricing page.

**OpenAI image generation via ChatGPT** is built into ChatGPT. OpenAI replaced DALL-E 3 with GPT-4o native image generation in ChatGPT; the exact transition date is not confirmed in official OpenAI documentation available at time of writing. The free tier includes some image generation with dynamic usage caps that OpenAI does not publish as a fixed number. ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) raises limits and adds priority access. OpenAI grants full commercial rights to image outputs, subject to content policy. If you already pay for ChatGPT Plus, this is a zero-additional-cost starting point. Note: according to third-party sources, the DALL-E 3 model was announced for deprecation in the API around mid-2026, though official confirmation of completed deprecation was not available in sources reviewed for this guide.

**Editorial judgment:** For a first-time user with no existing subscriptions, Firefly's free plan is the lowest-friction entry with the clearest commercial use policy. For output quality and stylistic range, Midjourney is consistently cited by working illustrators as the stronger tool, but the lack of a free trial is a real barrier.

tools.name: Adobe Firefly
tools.description: Browser-based image generation trained on licensed and public domain content. Integrated into Creative Cloud apps.
strengths: Clearest commercial use rights of the three options
Trained on licensed content, not open-web scrapes
Familiar interface for existing Creative Cloud users
limitations: Free tier is limited in credits and not licensed for commercial use
Output style is more conservative compared to Midjourney
Credit system adds complexity to understanding actual costs

tools.name: Midjourney
tools.description: High-quality image generation accessible via web interface or Discord. Widely used by professional illustrators and concept artists.
strengths: Consistently strong visual output across many styles
No longer requires Discord — standalone web interface available
Unlimited Relax Mode generations on Standard plan and above
Clear commercial use rights for paid subscribers
limitations: Basic plan's ~200 fast generations/month can run out quickly
Companies over $1M annual revenue must use Pro or Mega for commercial use
No public API as of mid-2026

tools.name: ChatGPT Image Generation (OpenAI)
tools.description: Image generation built into ChatGPT, now powered by GPT-4o native image generation rather than DALL-E 3.
strengths: Included in ChatGPT free and Plus subscriptions
Full commercial use rights granted by OpenAI
limitations: Free-tier image quota is dynamic and not publicly documented
Less control over fine-grained style parameters compared to Midjourney
DALL-E 3 (the prior model) is deprecated in the API as of mid-2026

sections.heading: Your First Prompt: A Simple Framework That Works
sections.body_markdown:
A prompt is just a description. But vague descriptions produce vague results. The fastest way to get useful output is to include four elements:

1. **Subject.** What is in the image? Be specific. "A woman" produces generic results. "A woman in her 40s looking out a rain-streaked window" gives the model more to work with.
2. **Style or medium.** Oil painting, product photo, flat vector illustration, pencil sketch, cinematic still. Without this, the tool picks a style for you, and it is often generic.
3. **Mood or lighting.** "Overcast morning light," "neon-lit," "warm late afternoon sun." Lighting descriptions have an outsized effect on the feel of the output.
4. **Composition or framing (optional but useful).** "Close-up portrait," "wide establishing shot," "bird's eye view."

**Example prompt (weak):** `a coffee shop`

**Example prompt (stronger):** `Interior of an independent coffee shop, morning light through large windows, warm tones, a few empty chairs, film photograph style, slightly grainy`

The stronger version takes 15 extra seconds to write and reliably produces more useful results. You are not writing code. Write the way you would describe an image to a collaborator.

sections.heading: Reading the Output: What Good and Bad Results Look Like
sections.body_markdown:
When you get your first results, evaluate them on these dimensions:

- **Does the composition serve your purpose?** Ignore imperfections and ask whether the core layout is usable.
- **Are there anatomical or structural errors?** Hands, text in images, and complex backgrounds are common failure points. Fingers that merge, illegible text, and distorted symmetrical objects (glasses, windows, wheels) are the most frequent artifacts.
- **Is the style consistent with your brief?** If you asked for "editorial photography" and got "fantasy illustration," your prompt needs more specificity, not a completely new prompt.

A useful result does not need to be perfect. For concepting, moodboarding, or reference, a 70% match is often enough to move forward. For final production use, these tools require post-processing or significant iteration.

sections.heading: Basic Controls: Aspect Ratio and Style Settings
sections.body_markdown:
Every major tool lets you set **aspect ratio** before generating. This matters more than most beginners expect. Generating a landscape-format image with a portrait ratio will always produce an awkward crop. Set the ratio to match your intended use:

- **1:1** for social media thumbnails and avatars
- **16:9** for video thumbnails, presentations, wide headers
- **2:3 or 4:5** for vertical social, print portrait
- **3:2** for standard photo print proportions

**Resolution:** On free tiers, output is typically at standard resolution, which is sufficient for screen use but may require upscaling for print. Specific pixel dimensions vary by tool and plan, and the platforms do not always publish these numbers clearly. Treat free-tier output as draft or screen-use quality until you verify what your specific plan produces.

**Style intensity or "stylize" settings (Midjourney):** Midjourney includes a `--stylize` parameter that controls how strongly the model applies its own aesthetic preferences. Lower values follow your prompt more literally. Higher values produce more visually elaborate results that may drift from your description. For beginners, leaving this at the default is fine until you understand how your prompts behave.

sections.heading: Iteration Workflow: How to Refine Without Starting Over
sections.body_markdown:
The most common beginner mistake is abandoning a generation entirely when it is 80% right. The more efficient approach:

1. **Generate 4 variations** from your first prompt. Most tools generate multiple options by default.
2. **Pick the closest result** and identify the one or two things that are wrong.
3. **Change one element of your prompt** and regenerate. Do not rewrite the entire prompt at once. You will lose what was working.
4. **Use the tool's variation or remix features** if they exist. Midjourney's "vary" feature generates alternatives close to a selected image. Firefly and ChatGPT support conversational iteration where you describe the change you want.

For text-in-image needs: add the exact text you need in quotes in your prompt (e.g., `sign reading "Open"`). Results are still unreliable across all three tools, but explicit instruction helps. For anything requiring precise, legible text, plan to add it in post-production.

sections.heading: Common Beginner Mistakes
sections.body_markdown:
**Over-prompting on the first try.** Writing a 200-word prompt before you understand how a tool responds to simple prompts wastes time. Start with 20 to 40 words, see what you get, then add specificity.

**Ignoring aspect ratio.** Generating the wrong ratio and then cropping is the leading cause of frustrated beginners who think the tool "does not work."

**Treating one bad generation as a verdict.** AI image generation is probabilistic. The same prompt produces different results each time. If something is not working, run it again or adjust one element before concluding the tool cannot do what you need.

**Using the free tier for client deliverables without checking the license.** Free-tier outputs from Adobe Firefly are not licensed for commercial use. Verify the commercial terms of whatever tier you are on before delivering AI-generated work to a client.

sections.heading: What These Tools Cannot Do
sections.body_markdown:
Honest limitations that affect practical use:

- **Reliable text rendering.** All three tools struggle with legible, correctly spelled text inside images. This is a known, documented limitation, not a prompt-writing problem.
- **Precise control over specific people or characters.** Without additional features (like Firefly Custom Models or Midjourney's reference image tools), you cannot reliably reproduce a specific face, character, or brand asset across multiple generations.
- **Hands and complex anatomy.** Distortion in hands, fingers, and feet is the most common structural artifact. Improving with each model generation, but still a consistent weak point.
- **Consistent output across a project.** Generating 20 images that feel like they belong to the same visual world requires significant prompt discipline and, often, paid features designed for consistency.
- **Copyright certainty.** Adobe Firefly's training data policy (trained on licensed and public domain content, not on subscribers' personal content per Adobe's documentation) is the most explicitly documented of the three. For Midjourney and OpenAI outputs, commercial use rights are granted by the platform, but what training data those models used is less publicly detailed, and the broader legal landscape around AI-generated imagery is still unsettled. This is an evolving area and not something any of these tools has fully resolved.

key_takeaways: No hardware or coding is required. All three major tools run in a browser, but none is fully free for unlimited use.
Adobe Firefly is the most accessible starting point with the clearest commercial use policy; Midjourney produces stronger stylistic output but has no free trial.
A prompt with a subject, style, mood, and framing will outperform a vague one almost every time. Start short and add specificity based on results.
Set aspect ratio before generating. It has an immediate, obvious effect on whether outputs are usable for your intended format.
Hands, in-image text, and cross-session character consistency are documented weak points across all three tools. Plan your workflow around these constraints rather than fighting them.
article.conclusion:
AI image generation is a genuinely useful tool for concepting, moodboarding, reference gathering, and in some cases final production, but it requires realistic expectations about what it produces on the first try. The gap between a vague prompt and a useful one is smaller than most beginners expect. The gap between a first generation and a polished deliverable is larger.

Pick one tool, spend 20 minutes with it, and evaluate based on your actual use case. The tools covered here are meaningfully different in cost structure, training data policy, and output style. Those differences matter depending on whether you need commercial rights, care about training data sourcing, or prioritize a specific visual quality. None of them is the right answer for every creator.
article.methodology_disclosure: This guide was synthesized from official documentation for Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, and OpenAI (accessed July 2026), supplemented by independent reviews from eesel.ai, ToolRadar, ToolColumn, Flowith Blog, AI Tool Radar, and Terms.Law. Claims about pricing, free tier limits, commercial rights, and platform capabilities were cross-referenced against official sources wherever possible. Where official documentation was ambiguous or silent (such as exact free-tier credit counts and pixel-dimension caps), the guide softens or omits specific figures rather than relying solely on third-party estimates. The guide does not reflect hands-on testing by dailyblip. Prompt framework recommendations and iteration advice are based on editorial synthesis of practitioner workflows described in sourced materials, not original testing.

issues.original_text: formerly DALL-E 3, now GPT-4o native image generation as of December 2025
issues.issue: The source (AI Tool Radar, an independent review) states DALL-E 3 was removed from ChatGPT in December 2025 and replaced with GPT-4o image generation, but this is an unverified third-party claim with no corroborating official OpenAI documentation in the source list. The article presents this transition date as established fact without an official source.

issues.original_text: DALL-E 3 model has been deprecated in the API as of mid-2026
issues.issue: The AI Tool Radar source states deprecation was 'announced for May 2026,' not confirmed as completed by mid-2026. The article asserts it is deprecated as of mid-2026 as a fact, but no official OpenAI documentation in the source list confirms this completed deprecation.

issues.original_text: free plan includes a limited monthly credit allocation. A third-party review cites approximately 25 credits
issues.issue: The article correctly notes Adobe's official docs don't specify a number, but the body text still leads with 'approximately 25 credits' sourced only from ToolRadar (an independent review). The article hedges this appropriately in text, but the tool card and other references treat credit counts as near-factual, when Adobe's own documentation explicitly does not confirm this figure.

issues.original_text: free-tier output is at standard resolution and is not licensed for commercial use
issues.issue: The ToolRadar source describes free-tier output as 'watermarked and low-resolution,' but the article omits the watermark claim entirely. If free outputs are watermarked, this is a significant practical limitation that the article fails to disclose to readers.

issues.original_text: Midjourney's case, there is no free tier at all as of mid-2026
issues.issue: The source (eesel.ai independent review) states the free trial was removed in March 2023. Presenting this as a mid-2026 status update implies recent change when it has been the case for over three years; this framing could mislead readers about the recency of the change, though the underlying fact is correct.

issues.original_text: entry price is $10/month (or $8/month billed annually) for the Basic plan, which gives you roughly 200 fast GPU generations per month
issues.issue: The eesel.ai source describes the Basic plan cap as '200-minute Fast cap (~200 images/month)' — the unit is GPU-hours/minutes, not a fixed image count. Generation counts per unit of GPU time vary by model and settings, so '~200 generations' is an approximation from a third-party source, not a guaranteed quota, and this nuance is lost in the article's presentation.

issues.original_text: Adobe trains Firefly on licensed and public domain content, not scraped web data
issues.issue: The Adobe product page source states Firefly is trained on 'licensed content and public domain content where copyright has expired' and that Adobe does not train on subscribers' personal content. The article's phrasing 'not scraped web data' is an editorial characterization not directly stated in Adobe's official documentation and may overstate the precision of Adobe's disclosed training data policy.

issues.original_text: in Midjourney's case, there is no free tier at all as of mid-2026
issues.issue: The article states 'None of the major tools offers a fully unlimited free plan' and then separately notes Midjourney has no free tier at all — these are inconsistent framings. The first implies all three have some free tier; the second correctly clarifies Midjourney has none. This contradiction could confuse readers about Midjourney's actual availability.

issues.original_text: Standard ($30/month, or $24 annual) and above add unlimited "Relax Mode" generations
issues.issue: The eesel.ai source specifies that only the Standard, Pro, and Mega plans include unlimited Relax Mode, and explicitly states the Basic plan has no Relax Mode. The article correctly notes this but the Midjourney tool card lists 'Unlimited Relax Mode generations on Standard plan and above' as a strength without clarifying this is unavailable on Basic — readers comparing the card to the text may be confused about what the $10 Basic plan includes.

issues.original_text: The standalone Firefly plan starts at $9.99/month
issues.issue: The ToolRadar source cites $9.99/month for the Firefly Standard standalone plan, but ToolColumn notes that as of August 2025, Creative Cloud plan structures were reorganized. There is no official Adobe pricing page in the source list confirming the current $9.99 standalone price, making this figure dependent solely on a third-party review.

issues.original_text: supplemented by independent reviews from eesel.ai, ToolRadar, ToolColumn, Flowith Blog, AI Tool Radar, and Terms.Law
issues.issue: Terms.Law is listed as a source for legal analysis of commercial use rights, but the article does not disclose that Terms.Law is a commercial legal services site, not an independent journalistic or academic outlet. Readers may weight its legal analysis differently knowing its commercial context.

issues.original_text: Adobe Firefly's training data policy (licensed and public domain content) is the clearest of the three
issues.issue: This comparative claim about Midjourney's and OpenAI's training data policies implies those policies are less clear or less protective, but the article does not present evidence about what Midjourney's or OpenAI's training data actually consists of. The comparison is presented as editorial fact without supporting sourced documentation on the other two tools' training data.

The article presents this transition date as established fact without an official source.
